text.title: Mutation Testing as the Agent Feedback Signal Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules grounding the test-mutation-feedback-loop methodology plus the `skip-this-methodology` safety rule. Each rule pairs a binary check with a sourced rationale.

rule.statement: Mutation testing MUST be scoped to changed files in the PR (diff scope), not the whole codebase. Full-codebase mutation runs once weekly at most.
rule.rationale: Whole-codebase mutation per PR balloons CI cost; diff scope keeps the signal tight to the change under review.

rule.statement: PRs MUST set two thresholds: `break` (typical 70%) blocks merge; `high` (typical 80%) is the goal. Below break → fail; between break and high → warn; above high → pass clean.
rule.rationale: A single hard threshold creates pass/fail cliffs; the two-tier band gives the agent a clear `good enough vs aspirational` signal.

rule.statement: Surviving mutants MUST be returned to the coding agent's next iteration as structured input (mutant ID + diff + which test SHOULD have killed it). The agent iterates until all mutants die or are tagged equivalent.
rule.rationale: Mutation as a one-way gate produces frustrated humans; mutation as feedback produces tests that get stronger over iterations.

rule.statement: Tagging a mutant as `equivalent` (no test can distinguish it) MUST include a written justification stored alongside the test. A reviewer signs off on equivalence claims.
rule.rationale: Without justification, `equivalent` becomes the escape hatch that silently lowers the threshold.

rule.statement: When new tests are added, the project-wide mutation score MUST NOT regress below the rolling 4-week baseline. CI gates merges that lower the baseline by > 1 point.
rule.rationale: Without a ratchet, mutation score drifts down as flaky equivalence claims accumulate.

rule.statement: Skip this methodology when the preconditions in `## Applies If` are not all true, or any item in `## Skip If` holds. The decision tree routes to this rule on those branches.
rule.rationale: A methodology that runs on the wrong input wastes tokens and produces misleading artifacts. The explicit skip rule is the safety valve.
